segments.text: Erica: Hey Ian, how far along are you on preparing the presentation on volcanoes next week?
Ian: Not much.
Erica: Maybe we can straighten things out first?
Ian: Sure.
Ian: I decided to start with shield volcano.
Ian: The name derives from its similarity in shape to a warrior's shield lying face up.
Ian: The Hawaiian shield volcanoes are the most famous examples.
Ian: Professor Joan mentioned that many scholars have already done quite a lot of research in this field.
Ian: We could retell conclusions from published papers.
Erica: I'm not sure.
Erica: I mean, is that even allowed?
Erica: I know we don't have to use first-hand data we collect, but copying others?
Ian: Come on, it's not like we are using their findings as our own.
Ian: The professor said it would be okay as long as we cited our sources.
Erica: Oh, okay.
Ian: Do you have any ideas about stratovolcanoes?
Erica: Stratovolcanoes, also known as composite volcanoes, develop over many thousands of years through repeated eruptions and other volcanic activity.
Erica: But the current information I have at hand is only very vague.
Erica: Fuller descriptions are needed for this.
Ian: I'm sure you can find them.
Erica: Alright then.
Erica: Leave that to me.
Erica: Have you read anything about rhyolite caldera complexes?
Ian: Yeah, they are the most explosive volcanoes on Earth and don't even look like volcanoes.
Ian: The origin of these volcanoes, like Yellowstone, is associated with a hotspot.
Erica: You've got most of the information correct.
Erica: Unfortunately, the last bit you mentioned is wrong.
Erica: The origin of this type of volcano remains a mystery.
Erica: The hotspot origin theory doesn't work for most other rhyolite calderas.
Ian: Right.
Ian: I'll change that part.
Ian: What about the position on monogenetic fields?
Erica: It's shocking to see the photos of these immense volcanoes.
Erica: There are numerous monogenetic fields in the American southwest and Mexico.
Erica: But I got all the information from a website without specifying its source.
Erica: We'll have to examine the content again later.
Ian: Okay.
Ian: I can help with that.
Erica: Lastly, we have cinder cones.
Erica: What do you have on that?
Ian: Well, this type of volcano is usually tiny to the other volcanoes and thus sounds less attractive.
Ian: Maybe we should leave it out; it seems pretty unimportant to me.
Erica: I agree.
Erica: It's impossible to cover everything.
Ian: Erica, can you work on the delivery part?
Ian: I don't want to do it anymore.
Erica: I'm okay with that.
Erica: But why is that?
Ian: I was marked so low for the last presentation and didn't even have a clue why.
Ian: Maybe it's because I didn't include enough data on it.
Erica: Well, I'm not sure about that.
Erica: I think maybe you did not prepare the topic well enough.
Erica: You tried to explain the contributing factor forming the Ring of Fire in the Pacific Ocean, but you just read everything out loud without fully comprehending it yourself.
Ian: Right.
Ian: That must have been it.
Erica: We also have to look for supporting materials to illustrate the formation of different volcanoes.
Ian: What about documentaries?
Erica: They can be an excellent way to illustrate the volcanic eruption, but that might be too long for our presentation.
Erica: We've only got 20 minutes.
Ian: Have you considered adding photos to our handouts?
Erica: That's an option.
Erica: But people may focus on these photos rather than paying attention to what we have to say.
Erica: Maybe we can search online for part of a film about volcanoes and add it to the end of our presentation.
Ian: That would be better.
Ian: So how should we start our presentation?
Erica: Maybe we can first introduce some terms like extinct volcanoes and dormant volcanoes.
Ian: Why is that?
Erica: Even though they are widely-accepted terms among ordinary people, academia wouldn't accept them because there are no set definitions.
Erica: For example, an extinct volcano would be one nobody expects to erupt again.
Erica: But there have been several eruptions from widely-acknowledged 'extinct' volcanoes.
Ian: That's an excellent way to start our presentation.
Ian: What did the professor say about your last presentation?
Erica: There is still room for improvement.
Erica: She said that I had an in-depth analysis and provided enough statistics, but I didn't carry on and discuss my take on it.
Ian: Right.
Ian: So maybe we could also discuss the impact volcanoes have on humans and our planet.
Erica: Yeah.
Erica: There's little doubt that volcanoes have been instrumental in wiping out countless species during major extinction events.
Erica: But volcanic activity also shapes new habitats, enriching the soil with nutrients that allow life to thrive.
Erica: People haven't realised that there are abundant mineral resources waiting to be exploited in volcanoes.
Ian: I agree.
Ian: So now we can...
